titleStmt.title: "Title of Work"
Glosses
titleStmt.funder: American Philosophical Society, Beinecke Library, Bibliographical Society of America, Blossman Foundation, Huntington Library, Louisiana Board of Regents, National Endowment for the Humanities, NINES, Pierpont Morgan Library, South Central Modern Language Association, and Southeastern Louisiana University

distributor.name: Southeastern Louisiana University Department of English

sourceDesc.p: Born digitally, prepared by David C. Hanson.

div.note: Sicilia is the largest island in the Mediterranean Sea and has been inhabited since the very ancient times, approximately 10,000 years ago. Its name is derived from the Sicels (Siculi) who lived in the eastern part of the island in the Iron Age. Its form is triangular; each of the extremities being terminated by a Promontory; one of which, anciently called Lilybaem, another, called Pachymun, and the third called Pelorum. It is about sixty-six leagues in length, and in breadth forty-five (Starke, Travels in Europe between the Years 1824 and 1828, 1-2).

Mariane Starke describes the Strait of Messina as a narrow body of water between Sicily and Italy. The two famous spots –Scylla and Charybdis – are found on opposite sides of this strait. It is about 3 km wide at its narrowest point and is renowned for its strong tidal currents and whirlpools, which have been avoided by a lot of sailors (Travels in Europe between the Years 1824 and 1828, 2).

Mount Etna is a famous volcano on the coast of Sicily. It is a land of remarkable natural wealth and beauty. Starke emphasizes its fertile fields and abundant flowers. It is also known for its productive pastures and seas full of fish. Etna has established its powerful and dramatic presence, creating a lasting impression due to its volcanic eruptions and lava flows (Travels in Europe between the Years 1824 and 1828, 2).

"Catania’s city falls" describes the destruction caused by Mouth Etna’s eruption. In the story “Little Merchants” in a collection, The Parent's Assistant by Maria Edgeworth (1767-1849), it is shown how children contribute to the life and economy of a city through small trades and industrious work. While Etna’s eruption illustrates its devastating effects on its entire communities, Edgeworth’s story represents the resilience and daily labor of young people in maintaining the function of urban life (Edgeworth, The Parent’s Assistant, 80).

Represents the destructive power of nature. Mount Etna’s continued eruptions can destroy cities and fertile land, leaving a devastating effect. In the other way around, the poem highlights the life and vegetation return, as “green verdure meets the eyes,” representing the resilience of the land and its people. It serves as an illustration where natural disasters and human activity coexist and communities can move forward despite he challenges posed by the environment.
